summary: DHH describes how Basecamp initially used an elaborate automated system to determine whether projects were active or inactive, based on recent activity and milestone status. This clever approach caused problems, especially since Basecamp's pricing tiers were based on active project count. The solution was to abandon the automated detection entirely and simply let customers manually set project status themselves. This led to removing code and simplifying the system, reinforcing the principle that software shouldn't try to be smarter than the user.
key_points: Basecamp's automated active/inactive project detection created more problems than it solved, especially around tier limits
Letting users explicitly control project status was simpler and more respectful than algorithmic guessing
Simplifying the feature meant removing code, which DHH frames as the most enjoyable part of programming
Software that assumes on behalf of users often causes inconvenience rather than providing value
money_quotes: Stop being clever about it. Just let the customers decide when a project is either active, on hold, or ready for the archives.
I was able to do a bunch of what I enjoy most about programming — remove code rendered obsolete by simplifications by a deeper understanding of the domain.
We had the simple answer all along, but refused to realize that we were doing harm in an attempt to do good.
Stop assuming on behalf of a human what cannot be assumed without inconvenience.
And I can tell you that I enjoyed yanking every single one of those methods and callbacks.
key_insight: Software should stop trying to be clever on behalf of users — when in doubt, give humans explicit control rather than building elaborate automated assumptions.
